title: When It’s Value vs. Growth, History Is on Value’s Side | Dimensional
gist: US value stocks (low relative price) have beaten growth by an average 4.0% annually since 1927, with premiums that tend to arrive fast and large; Dimensional argues a persistent, consistent value tilt is required to capture them.

insights.claim: Value stocks have outperformed growth stocks in the US by an average of 4.0% annually since 1927.
insights.speaker: Dimensional Fund Advisors
insights.so_what: Sets the long-run baseline expected reward for a value tilt; a benchmark against which any current value drawdown can be judged.
insights.quote: On average, value stocks have outperformed growth stocks by 4.0% annually in the US since 1927, as Exhibit 1 shows.

insights.claim: In years when value beat growth in US markets, the average value premium was nearly 15%, indicating premiums tend to arrive in large magnitudes when they show up.
insights.speaker: Dimensional Fund Advisors
insights.so_what: The skewed, lumpy nature of the payoff means missing the up-years is costly, arguing against tactically timing in and out of value.
insights.quote: in years when value outperformed growth, the average premium was nearly 15%.
insights.locator: Value premiums have often shown up quickly

insights.claim: The value premium data spans nearly a century of yearly observations (1927–2025) using Fama/French indices.
insights.speaker: Dimensional Fund Advisors
insights.so_what: The near-century sample length is the core credibility argument; note Fama and French are Dimensional board affiliates, so the source is not disinterested.
insights.quote: Yearly observations of premiums: value minus growth in US markets, 1927–2025

insights.claim: The value premium rests on the premise that stocks trading at lower prices relative to fundamentals like book equity have higher expected returns because paying less for a given set of future cash flows raises expected return.
insights.speaker: Dimensional Fund Advisors
insights.so_what: Frames value not as a market anomaly but as a discount-rate mechanic, implying the premium should persist as long as prices vary relative to fundamentals.
insights.quote: Value investing is based on the premise that paying less for a set of future cash flows is associated with a higher expected return.
insights.locator: Value investing is based on the premise

insights.claim: Capturing the value premium requires a consistent, persistent focus on value stocks rather than tactical entry, because outperformance appears quickly and cannot be reliably timed.
insights.speaker: Dimensional Fund Advisors
insights.so_what: Supports a strategic, always-on value allocation; investors who exit during weak stretches risk missing the concentrated up-years.
insights.quote: A consistent focus on value stocks is essential to capturing these outsize value premiums when they appear.
insights.locator: A consistent focus on value stocks

insights.claim: Dimensional concedes disappointing stretches for value emerge periodically but argues the underlying principle that lower relative prices lead to higher expected returns is unchanged.
insights.speaker: Dimensional Fund Advisors
insights.so_what: Signals how a value manager reframes underperformance to retain clients; watch whether the theoretical premise or realized returns is being leaned on when value lags.
insights.quote: While disappointing periods emerge from time to time, the principle that lower relative prices lead to higher expected returns remains the same.
